content.text: Why Validate Tool Arguments

The LLM generates tool arguments as a JSON string. Even with a clear schema, it can produce arguments that are syntactically valid JSON but semantically wrong: a string where an integer is required, an enum value with the wrong casing, a date in the wrong format, or a negative number where only positive values make sense. Executing a tool with bad arguments can corrupt data, cause confusing errors, or silently produce wrong results.
Validation sits between argument parsing and tool execution. Its job: confirm the arguments are typed correctly, within allowed ranges, and structurally complete before any side effect runs.
The LLM is a probabilistic text generator, not a type-safe compiler. Validation is the contract enforcement layer that the schema alone cannot provide.
Layer 1 — JSON Parsing

The first validation step is confirming the argument string is valid JSON. The LLM occasionally emits truncated or malformed JSON, especially for large argument objects or under token pressure. Always wrap the parse in a try/except and return the error as a tool result.
Layer 2 — Schema Validation with jsonschema
After JSON parsing, validate the argument object against the tool's parameter schema. The
jsonschema
library validates types, required fields, enum values, numeric ranges, and string patterns.
Layer 3 — Pydantic Validation

Pydantic combines parsing and validation in one step and supports field validators, cross-field checks, and automatic coercion. It is the most ergonomic option when you control the tool implementation.
Semantic Validation — Business Rules

Schema validation confirms types and ranges. Semantic validation enforces business rules that a JSON schema cannot express: the referenced record must exist, the date must not be in the past, the amount must not exceed the account balance.
Returning Validation Errors to the LLM

When validation fails, return a clear, actionable error as the tool result. The LLM reads this and may self-correct on its next tool call. The quality of the error message directly affects the LLM's ability to fix it.
Error quality
Example
"error": "Invalid input"
"error": "Validation failed"
"error": "department must be one of [finance, hr, legal, engineering, all], got \"Finance\""
"error": "department: \"Finance\" is not valid. Use lowercase: \"finance\". Valid values: finance, hr, legal, engineering, all"
Validation Pipeline — Complete Pattern
Combine all three layers into a single, composable validation pipeline. Each layer returns early on failure; the tool only executes when all layers pass.
Validation is the guard between LLM text and real-world effects.

JSON parsing catches malformed output. Schema validation catches type and constraint violations. Business validation catches domain-rule violations. Clear error messages let the LLM self-correct. Without these layers, a single bad argument can corrupt data silently.
